title: 10 best herbal teas for queasy stomach

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Chinese peony teas helps with queasy stomach because it has a unique ability to calm the digestive system. The tea contains flavonoids, which help to reduce inflammation in the gut and alleviate nausea. Additionally, the antioxidants present in the tea help to soothe the stomach lining, reducing discomfort and promoting relaxation. As a result, herbal Chinese peony teas can provide relief from queasy stomach symptoms, helping to restore digestive balance and promote overall well-being.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory property: The tea's anti-inflammatory compounds help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, which can alleviate nausea and discomfort associated with a queasy stomach.
Soothing property: The tea's soothing compounds have a calming effect on the digestive system, helping to settle stomach upset and ease symptoms of queasiness.
Digestive- regulating property: The tea contains compounds that regulate digestion, reducing symptoms of bloating, gas, and indigestion, which can contribute to feelings of queasiness.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the Chinese peony plant have anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in calming nausea and indigestion.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes of the Chinese peony plant contain starch and fiber, which can help soothe digestive issues and alleviate symptoms of queasy stomach.
remedy_recipe: Gather 3 tablespoons of dried paeonia lactiflora roots and 2 tablespoons of dried ginger rhizomes.
Boil 2 cups of water in a pot for 5 minutes.
Add the dried herbs to the boiling water and let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth and discard the solids.
Let the tea cool down slightly before drinking and enjoy within 30 minutes.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ginger teas helps with queasy stomach because it contains bioactive compounds like gingerol and shogaol, which have natural anti-inflammatory and digestive properties. These compounds work to soothe the stomach lining, reducing inflammation and spasms that can cause nausea and discomfort. Additionally, ginger's carminative properties help to eliminate gas and bloating, further alleviating queasy feelings. By promoting digestion and calming the stomach, herbal ginger teas provide a natural and effective way to ease queasiness and find relief from stomach upset.
remedy_properties: Gingerol: Gingerols are bioactive compounds found in ginger that have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help to reduce nausea and alleviate digestive discomfort by relaxing the muscles in the stomach.

Shogaol: Shogaol is another bioactive compound present in ginger that has analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects, making it effective in reducing pain and inflammation associated with queasy stomach, such as those experienced during morning sickness or motion sickness.

6-Gingerol: 6-Gingerol is a minor constituent of ginger that has been shown to have strong anti-nausea properties, which can help to reduce vomiting and alleviate queasiness by stimulating the release of neurotransmitters that regulate stomach motility.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: Rhyzomes, or underground stems, of ginger have anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe digestive issues and reduce nausea.
Buds: Fresh ginger buds contain compounds like gingerol and shogaol, which are natural anti-emetics that can alleviate queasiness and stomach discomfort.
remedy_recipe: Gather 2-3 slices of fresh ginger root about 1/4 inch thick and cut them into small pieces.
Boil 1 liter of water in a large pot for 5-7 minutes then reduce heat to simmer.
Add the ginger pieces to the boiling water and steep for 5-10 minutes or until desired strength.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup discarding solids.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey or lemon juice if desired and drink immediately to soothe queasy stomach.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Chamomile teas helps with queasy stomach because it contains apigenin, an antioxidant that calms the digestive system. The soothing properties of chamomile help to reduce inflammation and relax the muscles in the stomach, allowing for easier digestion and reducing symptoms of nausea and vomiting. Additionally, chamomile's gentle anti-inflammatory effects can help to alleviate digestive discomfort and promote a sense of calm, making it an effective natural remedy for queasy stomachs.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Chamomile's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as apigenin and luteolin, can help soothe an upset stomach and reduce inflammation, which can contribute to digestive discomfort.
Antispasmodic properties: Chamomile's antispasmodic compounds can help relax the muscles in the stomach and intestines, reducing spasms and cramps that can exacerbate queasiness.
Anxiolytic (anxiety-reducing) properties: Chamomile has been shown to have a calming effect on the nervous system, which can help reduce stress and anxiety-related nausea and digestive discomfort.
remedy_parts: Roots: Used to calm digestive issues and reduce inflammation in the stomach, making them effective in soothing queasy stomach symptoms.
Flowers: Known for their anti-inflammatory properties, the flowers of the chamomile plant help to relax the muscles in the stomach and reduce nausea.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried matricaria chamomilla flowers in sufficient quantity for brewing.
Rinse the flowers gently with filtered water to remove any impurities or debris.
Combine one teaspoon of dried chamomile flowers or two teaspoons of fresh flowers with boiling water in a teapot.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes, allowing the flavonoids and apigenin to infuse into the tea.
Strain the tea into a cup using a tea strainer or cheesecloth and serve hot or chilled as desired.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Oats teas helps with queasy stomach because of its soothing properties that calm digestive issues. The oat's soluble fiber, beta-glucan, absorbs excess stomach acid and soothes irritated tissues, reducing inflammation and discomfort. Additionally, the tea's gentle warmth relaxes the digestive system, easing symptoms like nausea and bloating. As the tea works its magic, it also helps to regulate bowel movements, promoting a sense of balance and harmony in the body. With regular consumption, herbal oats teas can provide long-term relief from queasy stomach issues.
remedy_properties: Soothing Anti-Inflammatory Compounds: Oat tea contains avenanthramides, which have anti-inflammatory and soothing effects on the digestive tract, helping to calm upset stomachs and reduce nausea.
Fiber-Rich Soluble Fiber: Oats are high in soluble fiber, which helps to absorb and remove toxins and excess acids from the stomach, reducing discomfort and queasiness associated with indigestion.

Antioxidant Properties: The antioxidants present in oat tea, such as avenalumin, help to protect the digestive tract lining from damage caused by free radicals, reducing inflammation and irritation that can contribute to queasy stomachs.
remedy_parts: Roots: Oat roots have anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in soothing digestive issues and reducing nausea.
Barks: The bark of oat plants contains compounds that can help calm irritations in the stomach lining, providing relief from queasiness.
remedy_recipe: Harvest avena sativa leaves and flowers in the morning after dew has dried for freshness.
Rinse 1 teaspoon of dried or 2 teaspoons of fresh avena sativa herbs thoroughly under cold running water.
Boil 1 cup of filtered water in a pot then add rinsed avena sativa herbs allowing steeping for 5 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids immediately after steeping.
Drink the warm herbal tea slowly while sitting comfortably for 10-15 minutes before eating or taking medication.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Maypop teas helps with queasy stomach because of its unique combination of flavonoids, terpenes, and alkaloids that work together to soothe and calm the digestive system. The tea's gentle anti-inflammatory properties help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, while its calming effects on the nervous system ease anxiety and stress-related symptoms. Additionally, maypop tea's natural antispasmodic properties relax the muscles in the stomach, reducing cramps and discomfort associated with queasy stomachs.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Maypop tea's antioxidants help protect the digestive tract from damage caused by free radicals, which can contribute to queasiness and discomfort in the stomach.
Anti-inflammatory properties: Maypop tea's anti-inflammatory compounds can help reduce inflammation in the stomach, which may be causing nausea, bloating, or abdominal pain.

Soothing and calming effects: The flavonoids and other bioactive compounds present in maypop tea have a calming effect on the digestive system, helping to ease queasiness, cramps, and spasms that can come with an upset stomach.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the Maypop plant are used to create a tea that helps soothe and calm an upset stomach, reducing nausea and vomiting.
Barks: The barks of the Maypop plant contain compounds that help to neutralize digestive acids, providing relief from stomach discomfort and queasiness.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh passiflora incarnata leaves and flowers or purchase dried herbs from a reputable source for a total of one teaspoon.
Measure one teaspoon of dried maypop leaves and flowers into a medium-sized heat-resistant cup or mug.
Boil 8 ounces of pure water in a kettle or on the stove and pour it over the measured maypop herb.
Allow the mixture to steep for 5-7 minutes to release the active compounds and beneficial properties.
Strain the tea into another cup using a fine-mesh strainer or cheesecloth and discard the solids before serving.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Hawthorn teas helps with queasy stomach because it possesses a unique combination of properties that soothe digestive discomforts. The tea's flavonoids, anthocyanins, and quercetin work together to calm the stomach lining, reducing inflammation and irritation. Hawthorn's bitter compounds also stimulate digestion, helping to break down food more efficiently. Additionally, its mild anti-inflammatory effects may help alleviate nausea and vomiting associated with queasy stomachs. As a result, hawthorn tea can provide gentle relief for those experiencing digestive distress.
remedy_properties: Flavonoids: Hawthorn tea contains flavonoids, which have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the stomach and intestines, alleviating symptoms of queasiness.
Tannins: Tannins present in hawthorn tea can help tighten and calm the muscles in the digestive tract, reducing spasms and discomfort that often accompany queasy stomachs.
Antioxidants: Hawthorn tea's high antioxidant content can help neutralize free radicals and reduce oxidative stress in the gut, which can contribute to nausea and vomiting.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in flavonoids and saponins, which help to soothe digestive issues and reduce inflammation.
Flowers: Contain bioactive compounds that have anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in calming upset stomachs.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried hawthorn berries and leaves from trusted sources.
Combine 1 teaspoon of dried hawthorn leaves and 1/2 teaspoon of dried hawthorn berries with 8 ounces of boiling water.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the active compounds to infuse into the tea.
Strain the tea through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove solids.
Enjoy the tea hot or warm, allowing it to cool if needed, and consume within 2 hours.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Fennel teas helps with queasy stomach because it contains a unique combination of volatile oils, including anethole, which has natural anti-inflammatory properties. These oils help to soothe and calm the digestive tract, reducing inflammation and discomfort that can cause queasiness. Additionally, fennel's gentle stimulant properties help to ease bloating and gas, allowing the body to digest food more efficiently and alleviating symptoms of an upset stomach. As a result, fennel teas provide fast and effective relief from queasy stomachs.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Fennel tea contains anti-inflammatory compounds like anethole, which can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate symptoms of nausea and queasiness.
Gastrointestinal soothing properties: Fennel tea has been shown to have a calming effect on the muscles in the stomach, reducing spasms and cramps that can contribute to queasy feelings.

Carminative and antispasmodic effects: Fennel tea's carminative properties help to break down and eliminate gas and bloating, while its antispasmodic effects reduce muscle contractions that can lead to nausea and discomfort.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of fennel have been traditionally used to calm digestive issues and alleviate nausea due to their natural anti-inflammatory properties.
Seeds: Fennel seeds contain carvone, which has natural antispasmodic effects that help relieve digestive discomfort and ease queasy stomach symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried fennel leaves and fronds from a reputable source to ensure optimal potency and quality.
Measure out one to two teaspoons of dried fennel leaves per cup of boiling water for a strong brew.
Combine the measured fennel leaves with 250-300 milliliters of boiling water in a medium-sized pot or teapot.
Steep the mixture for seven to ten minutes, allowing the herbal properties to infuse and release their soothing benefits.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth, discarding the solids and serving warm or at room temperature.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Licorice teas helps with queasy stomach because it contains glycyrrhizin, a natural compound that soothes digestive issues. The tea's anti-inflammatory properties calm upset stomachs and reduce nausea, while its mucilaginous texture coats the stomach lining, providing relief from irritation and discomfort. Additionally, licorice teas have been shown to neutralize acid reflux and ease digestion, making them an effective remedy for alleviating queasy stomach sensations and promoting a sense of well-being.
remedy_properties: Glycyrrhizin: This compound has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help soothe and calm an irritated stomach lining, reducing nausea and discomfort.
Liquiritin: This flavonoid has been shown to have a sedative effect on the digestive system, which can help reduce spasms and cramps in the stomach and intestines that can contribute to queasiness.

Amiddens: The ammidens present in licorice root tea have been found to inhibit the production of histamine, a chemical released during allergic reactions, which can help alleviate symptoms of nausea and vomiting caused by food allergies or intolerances.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice plant contain glycyrrhizin, which helps soothe and calm digestive issues, making them an effective remedy for queasiness.
Barks: The barks of the licorice plant have been traditionally used to treat stomach problems, including nausea and vomiting, due to their anti-inflammatory properties.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried licorice root powder into a heat-resistant cup or teapot.
Boil one cup of filtered water in a kettle and pour it over the licorice root powder.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes, allowing the flavors to meld and the active compounds to release.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a separate cup to remove any solids.
Enjoy the warm tea immediately, taking slow sips as needed to help soothe an upset stomach.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Dandelion teas helps with queasy stomach because of its unique composition. The tea's bitter compounds, such as taraxasterol and taraxanthin, help to stimulate digestion and relieve symptoms of nausea and indigestion. Additionally, the tea's anti-inflammatory properties may help to soothe the digestive tract, reducing inflammation and discomfort. As a natural diuretic, dandelion tea can also help to alleviate bloating and reduce stomach pressure, providing relief for queasy stomachs.
remedy_properties: Bitter compounds: Dandelion tea's bitter compounds stimulate digestive enzymes and increase saliva production, which can help alleviate nausea and vomiting by promoting healthy digestion.

Inulin fiber: Inulin, a type of soluble fiber found in dandelion roots, helps soothe the stomach lining, reduce inflammation, and regulate bowel movements, making it an effective remedy for queasy stomachs.

Kaempferol: Kaempferol, a flavonoid antioxidant present in dandelion tea, has anti-inflammatory properties that may help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, which can contribute to queasiness and discomfort.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in bitter compounds, the roots help stimulate digestive enzymes and alleviate symptoms of indigestion.
Fruits: The fruits' high concentration of fiber helps soothe and regulate bowel movements, reducing nausea and discomfort.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh dandelion roots and leaves from a pesticide-free area or purchase dried organic herbs.
Measure one teaspoon of dried dandelion root and two teaspoons of dried dandelion leaves for every eight ounces of boiling water.
Combine the measured ingredients in a heat-resistant cup or teapot and pour in boiling water, allowing it to steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into another cup, discarding the solids and reserving the liquid.
Drink the tea immediately after straining, enjoying the soothing and calming effects on queasy stomachs within 30-45 minutes.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Elder teas helps with queasy stomach because of its natural soothing properties that calm digestive issues. remedy_properties: Antispasmodic properties: Elder tea's antispasmodic properties help to relax the muscles in the digestive tract, reducing cramps and spasms that can contribute to queasiness.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds in elder tea, such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, may help reduce inflammation in the stomach and intestines, which can alleviate symptoms of queasy stomach like nausea and bloating.

Astringent properties: Elder tea's astringent properties can help reduce inflammation and irritation in the digestive tract, making it easier for the stomach to digest food and reducing discomfort and queasiness.
remedy_parts: Roots: Elder roots have been traditionally used to calm digestive issues, including nausea and indigestion, due to their anti-inflammatory and soothing properties.
Barks: The barks of elder plants contain a high concentration of flavonoids and tannins that help to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate symptoms of queasy stomach.
remedy_recipe: Harvest dried elder flowers in amounts of one to two teaspoons per cup.
Boil filtered water in a pot for five minutes to create a temperature ideal for steeping.
Combine the dried elder flowers with boiling water and allow it to steep for seven to ten minutes.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a clean cup and discard the solids.
Serve the tea immediately and enjoy after preparing in under twenty minutes with one serving.

intro_desc: Herbal teas for queasy stomach are a natural and gentle remedy to soothe and calm digestive issues, providing relief from nausea, bloating, and discomfort. These teas work by promoting relaxation, reducing inflammation, and aiding digestion, ultimately alleviating symptoms of an upset stomach. Examples of herbal teas that help with queasy stomach include peppermint, ginger, and fennel, which can be consumed hot or cold. For instance, sipping on a warm cup of peppermint tea before a morning flight can significantly reduce travel-induced nausea, allowing for a more enjoyable journey.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat queasy stomach with herbal teas is to brew a soothing cup of peppermint tea. Peppermint's calming properties help ease digestive discomfort and reduce nausea. Ginger tea is also effective in alleviating queasiness due to its natural anti-inflammatory compounds that aid digestion. Other options include chamomile, licorice root, and fennel teas, which promote relaxation and ease stomach upset.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to queasy stomach that are treated with herbal teas are digestive issues such as bloating, cramps, and indigestion. Herbal teas like peppermint, ginger, and fennel help soothe the stomach and alleviate symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), nausea, and acid reflux. These teas also ease menstrual cramps and morning sickness during pregnancy. Additionally, they may be used to treat food poisoning, upset stomach, and stomach flu.
